title: Applying confidentiality rules to 2023 Census data and summary of changes since 2018 and 2013 Censuses | Stats NZ
content:
## Applying confidentiality rules to 2023 Census data and summary of changes since 2018 and 2013 Censuses
16 April 2024, 1:00pm
Applying confidentiality rules to 2023 Census data and summary of changes since 2018 and 2013 Censuses explains the confidentiality rules and procedures for the 2023 Census and summarises changes since the 2013 Census and 2018 Census.

## Download document and examples of tables
- Applying confidentiality rules to 2023 Census data and summary of changes since 2018 and 2013 Censuses Adobe Acrobat PDF file, 728 KB

### Applying confidentiality rules to 2023 Census data and summary of changes since 2018 and 2013 Censuses
Adobe Acrobat PDF file, 728 KB
- 2023 Census confidentiality rules: Example tables Excel spreadsheet, 69 KB

### 2023 Census confidentiality rules: Example tables
Excel spreadsheet, 69 KB
- Summary of key points
- About the 2023 Census confidentiality rules
- 2023 Census confidentiality rules
- How we apply confidentiality rules
- Summary of main changes from 2013 to 2023
- Detail of main changes from 2013 to 2023
- How we reviewed the confidentiality rules

## Summary of key points
For the 2018 Census, we reviewed and updated the 2013 Census confidentiality rules based on internal and external feedback. We developed seven rules with the aim of ensuring the safety of outputs while maximising data utility for our customers.
For the 2023 Census, we have retained the same rules as for the 2018 Census, but we have made a range of improvements to their implementation, based on a range of requirements gathered from 2018 Census data users. These changes will make it easier to produce 2023 Census outputs, including customised data requests.
Rules 1 and 2 are decision rules that determine whether a table is ‘sensitive’ (tables that are sensitive have their small-value cells suppressed by rule 3, while tables that are not sensitive do not), and rule 3 suppresses small values in cells in sensitive tables. Rule 4 adds ‘noise’ (the amount that is added or subtracted from the true value to confidentialise it) to the cells and is always applied. Rule 5 is applied to proportions, ratios, and percentages. Rule 6 decides when to suppress a measure. Rule 7 applies noise to unsuppressed measures.
The overall effect of these rules is that all tables of counts can be released, but some of them (those deemed sensitive by rule 1 or rule 2) will have counts below six suppressed. All remaining counts for release are randomly rounded to base 3 to add noise.
The primary changes since the 2013 Census are:
- the meshblock rule is no longer applicable, as Statistical area 1 geographies (SA1) are now the smallest standard output geography
- random rounding is now ‘fixed’ (that is, made consistent across different queries) to ensure consistency
- measures are now calculated from unrounded counts, with noise added afterwards; previously measures were calculated from rounded counts
- rounding of measures has been removed as a confidentiality rule because it is not done for confidentiality reasons
- release under license has been discontinued
- Methodological standard for confidentiality in the 2023 Census has been released to the public.

## About the 2023 Census confidentiality rules
The Data and Statistics Act 2022 requires Stats NZ and its employees to protect the identity of individuals and organisations contained within statistical outputs. To achieve this, we use a variety of confidentiality techniques to protect individuals and organisations from harm and to maintain public trust in our work.
For the 2018 Census, we made changes to the 2013 Census confidentiality rules based on internal and external review and consultation. We developed seven rules for the 2018 Census, with the aim of ensuring the safety of outputs while maximising data utility for our customers. These same rules are used for 2023 Census.
Our enduring goals for confidentiality are:
- utility – maximising the use of data
- safety – maintaining sufficient levels of confidentiality protection
- simplicity – providing procedures that can be efficiently described, understood, automated, and implemented
- consistency – providing consistent procedures and methodology across collections and modes of output.
The rules are consistent with a body of legal, professional, and ethical frameworks. This includes, for example, the frameworks we use from, and the relationships we have with, New Zealand government legislation and regulations, the United Nations, the International Statistical Institute, the New Zealand Statistical Association, and the Open Data Charter organisation.
These rules are also consistent and aligned with the Stats NZ’s framework guiding ethical and culturally appropriate data use in the Integrated Data Infrastructure (or IDI), for example tapu tikanga (protection protocols).
It is also consistent and aligned with the kaitiakitanga (guardianship) relationship principle of the Mana Ōrite Relationship Agreement signed between Stats NZ and the Data Iwi Leaders Group of the National Iwi Chairs Forum in 2019. These rules treat all 2023 Census individual unit record data used to produce 2023 Census release outputs with the same confidentiality practices as if the data were taonga (treasures, prized possessions).
Ngā Tikanga Paihere guidelines provides more information on the safe, responsible, and culturally appropriate use of data in the IDI.
Mana Ōrite Relationship Agreement provides more information on Stats NZ’s partnerships with the Data Iwi Leaders Group.
There are seven main confidentiality rules for the 2023 Census, as well as some supporting rules for special types of data. Rules 1 and 2 are decision rules that determine whether a table is sensitive, and rule 3 suppresses small cells in sensitive tables. Rule 4 adds noise to the cells and is always applied. Rule 5 is applied to proportions, ratios, and percentages. Rule 6 decides when to suppress a measure. Rule 7 applies noise to unsuppressed measures.
The overall effect of applying these rules is that all tables of counts can be released, but some of them (those deemed sensitive by rule 1 or rule 2) will have counts below six suppressed. All remaining counts for release are randomly rounded to base 3 to add noise.
When we refer to a ‘table’ for rules 1 to 3, we mean a very specific type of table – a dimensional table for a single geographic area. If a customer requests a table with two variables (for example, ‘Age’ by ‘Sex’), then this table can be considered to contain several different dimensional tables (for example, a one-dimensional ‘Age’ table, a one-dimensional ‘Sex’ table, and a two-dimensional ‘Age’ by ‘Sex’ table).
Figure 1: An example of a table for rules 1 to 3, using ‘Work and labour force status’, ‘Sex at birth’, and statistical area geographic data
Note: Pop = population; M = male; F = female ; SA = statistical area .
The first data row of this output table contains:
- one two-dimensional table – ‘Work and labour force status’ by ‘Sex at birth’.
- three one-dimensional tables – ‘Work and labour force status’, ‘Sex at birth’, and the subject population total.
Each of the following are treated as an individual table for rules 1 to 3:
- The cells highlighted in blue are a table – this is the two-dimensional table (‘Work and labour force status’ by ‘Sex at birth’) for SA #1.
- The cells highlighted in green are a table – this is the one-dimensional table (‘Work and labour force status’) for SA #1.
- The cells highlighted in orange are a table – this is the one-dimensional table (‘Sex at birth’) for SA #1.
- The cell highlighted in purple is a table – this is the subject population total for SA #1.
- The same would apply for SA #2 and SA #3.
Text alternative for Figure 1: An example of a table for rules 1 to 3, using ‘Work and labour force status’, ‘Sex at birth’, and statistical area geographic data
We apply these rules separately to the tables for each geographic area. ‘Geographic area’ is a generic term used to describe the various standard Stats NZ output geographies, such as Statistical area 1 geographies (SA1) and Statistical area 2 geographies (SA2), territorial authority areas, and regional council areas.
The geographies covered by the standard 2023 Census confidentiality rules are:
- single standard output geographies at SA1 level or larger
- standard and non-standard groupings of standard output geographies at SA2 level or larger
- standard groupings of meshblocks and SA1 geographies, which are limited to: SA2 geographies, SA3 geographies, urban rural geographies territorial authorities, regional councils electoral boundaries: ward, community board (includes local board), territorial authority subdivision, regional council constituency, or Māori constituency general electoral district or Māori electoral district police station, police area, or police district district health boards or other health-related geographies that replace them statistical areas or other legal or government boundaries as approved by the census general manager
- SA2 geographies, SA3 geographies, urban rural geographies
- territorial authorities, regional councils
- electoral boundaries: ward, community board (includes local board), territorial authority subdivision, regional council constituency, or Māori constituency general electoral district or Māori electoral district
- ward, community board (includes local board), territorial authority subdivision, regional council constituency, or Māori constituency
- general electoral district or Māori electoral district
- police station, police area, or police district
- district health boards or other health-related geographies that replace them
- statistical areas or other legal or government boundaries as approved by the census general manager
- subject population totals of meshblocks.
The rules are applied to all census data we release and have been automated in the data output systems where possible. If the need for any exceptions arises, these will be documented and made available on the Stats NZ website.
These 2023 Census confidentiality rules are used to produce tables of counts from 2023 Census data, and the proportions and measures derived from them. They do not cover all the requirements for protecting unit record data (or ‘microdata’), because access to the Integrated Data Infrastructure (IDI) and 2023 Census microdata is granted to approved researchers through the Stats NZ Data Lab. Data Lab processes protect the microdata and regulate outputs in ways that are similar to these rules, but sometimes favour safety over utility.
Microdata output guide , for example, can potentially have data releases that are safer but release less data than data releases using these 2023 Census confidentiality rules.
Apply to use microdata for research has more information.

## 2023 Census confidentiality rules

### Rule 1: Geography variable rule
The purpose of this rule is to identify sensitive-skewed tables where small groups or individuals can be identified. This rule asks whether two or more geographic variables from the following list are present in a table:
- Census night address
- Usual residence one year ago
- Usual residence five years ago
- Educational institute address.
If two more of these geographic variables are present, the table is deemed sensitive and requires suppression of small cells by rule 3.
View the Excel file under Download document and examples of tables for:
- Table 1: Example of a table passing rule 1, geography variable rule (table using one geographic variable and one geographic area)
- Table 2: Example of a table passing rule 1, geography variable rule (table using one geographic variable and two geographic areas)
- Table 3: Example of a table failing rule 1, geography variable rule (table using two geographical variables).

### Rule 2: Mean cell size rule
The purpose of this rule is to identify sensitive sparse tables where small groups or individuals can be identified, by looking at the ‘mean cell size’ of each table.
The mean cell size is applied separately at each geographic level and is calculated as shown below.
Text alternative for Mean cell size equation diagram
A table is sensitive if the mean cell size is less than or equal to 2. We then apply rule 3 to each sensitive table for each geographic unit.

#### Exceptions to rule 2
A table may contain the total count for its subject population by geographic area only, and not use any other variables. Such a table is not subject to rule 2 and is treated as not sensitive. Rule 3 is therefore not required, and only rule 4 needs to be applied.
View the Excel file under Download document and examples of tables for:
- Table 4: Example of a table passing and failing rule 2, mean cell size rule (table using one variable)
- Table 5: Example of a table passing and failing rule 2, mean cell size rule (table using two variables).

### Rule 3: Threshold rule
When the table for a geographic unit is deemed sensitive by either rule 1 or rule 2, all counts of less than six are suppressed.
The purpose of this rule is to enhance both utility and safety. The larger and more useful counts of six or more can always be released. The smaller counts are protected, where tables are likely to be sparse or related to small subject populations.
View the Excel file under Download document and examples of tables for:
- Tables 3 to 5: Examples of suppressed sensitive cells that have failed rule 1 or rule 2.

### Rule 4: Perturbation rule
Perturbation (the altering of cells’ values) is performed by applying ‘fixed random rounding’ to base 3 (FRR3). ‘Fixed’ means that random rounding is applied consistently across different tables. ‘Random rounding to base 3’ involves randomly rounding every unsuppressed count in a table to a number divisible by three.
We apply fixed random rounding to base 3 to census tables, by rounding values to:
- the nearest multiple of three with a probability of two-thirds (applied approximately two-thirds of the time)
- the second-closest multiple of three with a probability of one-third (applied approximately one-third of the time).
We do not change values that are already multiples of three.
We apply fixed random rounding of all counts to base 3, large and small, to:
- disguise small counts of 0, 1, and 2, which have the highest disclosure risk
- protect against the recalculation of small counts by subtracting from large counts (called ‘differencing’)
- retain almost all the statistical properties of the original table by adding only a little noise to the larger counts
- allow production of a wide range of census products and services tables and customised data request tables, which are pre-confidentialised tables where variables are selected and filtered, then confidentialised, and then published.
Each value in a table is rounded, including the totals. This means that the marginal totals can differ slightly from the corresponding sum of the rows or columns. That is, if the columns or rows in a table are added, they will not always equal the total given. Almost all other statistical properties of tables are retained, and values will never be more than two counts higher or lower than their original number.
View the Excel file under Download document and examples of tables for:
- Table 6: Example of a table using rule 4, perturbation rule.

### Rule 5: Proportions from counts rule
The purpose of this rule is to prevent the reconstruction of the original unrounded counts.
All proportions, percentages, and ratios (not totals) that come from counts must be calculated using fully confidentialised data. A proportion will only be available for release if both counts in the proportion have not been suppressed.

### Rule 6: Suppression for measures rule
A measure is suppressed if the total unrounded count of individuals contributing to the measure is less than the threshold value for that type of measure.
For each measure there is a threshold value:
- for means and medians this threshold value is six, so any table with less than six individuals/households/families contributing to it will not have a median or mean shown
- for quartiles the threshold is 12
- for quintiles it is 15
- for deciles it is 30.

### Rule 7: Noise for measures rule
The purpose of this rule is to prevent the reconstruction of the original unrounded counts. Means, medians, and other quantiles (but not proportions, which are covered by rule 5) are calculated from raw, unperturbed counts and have a fixed amount of random noise applied to them. ‘Fixed’ means that the noise is applied consistently across different tables. ‘Noise’ is a value that is added or subtracted from the true measure to confidentialise it. The noise is calculated differently depending on the type of measure.

## How we apply confidentiality rules
Figure 2: The decision process for confidentialising tables of counts (rules 1 to 4)
Text alternative for Figure 2: The decision process for confidentialising tables of counts (rules 1 to 4)
Figure 3: The decision process for confidentialising outputs containing proportions or measures (rules 5 to 7)
Text alternative for Figure 3 : T he decision process for confidentialising output s containing proportions or measures (rules 5 to 7)

## Detail of main changes from 2013 to 2023

### Geographic variables rule changes

#### 2013 – Rule 1: Meshblock rule
If the geographic unit for the table is a meshblock, and if the table uses a variable that is at a level more detailed than its highest level, or one that uses two or more variables, then the table is deemed to be sensitive. Then rule 3, the threshold rule, will apply to the table.

#### 2018, 2023 – Rule 1: Geographic variables rules
If a table contains two or more geographic variables, then the table is deemed to be sensitive. Then rule 3, the threshold rule, is applied to sensitive tables.
Geographic variables for the 2023 Census are:
- Census night address
- Usual residence one year ago
- Usual residence five years ago
- Educational institute address.

#### Explanation of changes
We dropped the meshblock rule in the 2018 Census. This was possible because census now uses SA1s as the smallest output geography, replacing meshblocks. The SA1 geography groups together the smallest meshblocks, providing a more consistently sized output geography than the meshblock geography.
Dropping rule 1 means that SA1 tables, which include category variables at a detailed level, or with more than one variable, are not automatically deemed as being sensitive. Single meshblock outputs are no longer covered by the standard census confidentiality rules, except for standard subject population totals.
The 2018 and 2023 geographic variable rule was previously part of 2013 Census rule 2, the mean cell size rule. This geographic variable rule has been split off as its own rule in 2018 and 2023 (see below). This has the effect of identifying sensitive-skewed tables, where potentially small groups of individuals could be identified.

### Mean cell size rule changes

#### 2013, 2018, 2023 – Rule 2: Mean cell size rule
A table is defined as sensitive if the mean cell size is equal to or less than two. The mean cell size is calculated as the total unrounded subject population of a geographic area, divided by the number of categories for that same geographic area.

#### Explanation of changes

### Threshold rule changes

#### 2013, 2018, 2023 – Rule 3: Threshold rule
When the table for a geographic unit is deemed sensitive by either rule 1 or rule 2, all counts less than six are suppressed.

#### Explanation of changes

### Perturbation rule changes

#### 2013 – Rule 4: Random rounding rule
All counts for individuals, families, households, and dwellings must be (independently) randomly rounded to base 3 (RR3).

#### 2018, 2023 – Rule 4: Perturbation rule
All counts for individuals, families, households, and dwellings must have fixed/consistent random rounding to base 3 (FRR3) applied.

#### Explanation of changes
The perturbation method has changed from RR3 in the 2013 Census, to FRR3 in the 2018 Census and 2023 Census. This means that identical table cell outputs will be rounded in the same consistent manner, regardless of source or output tool. This rule makes different outputs consistent, increases data utility, and makes data easier for customers to understand.

### Proportions from counts rule changes

#### 2013, 2018, 2023 – Rule 5: Proportions from counts rule
Proportions are calculated from perturbed counts and suppressed if any contributing count is suppressed.

#### Explanation of changes

### Suppression for measures rule changes

#### 2013, 2018, 2023 – Rule 6: Suppression for measures rule
Suppress a measure if the total unrounded count of individuals contributing to that measure are below a specified threshold for that measure:
- for medians and means the threshold is six
- for quartiles it is 12
- for quintiles it is 15
- for deciles it is 30.

#### Explanation of changes

### Noise for measures rule changes

#### 2013 – Rule 7: Use of rounded counts for calculating measures rule
All measures need to be calculated from randomly rounded counts.

#### 2018, 2023 – Rule 7: Noise for measures rule
Measures are calculated from unperturbed counts, and random noise is applied.

#### Explanation of changes
The 2013 Census rule 7 compounded and exaggerated RR3 noise, producing some measures that were far from their true value. This has been changed since the 2018 Census by calculating measures directly from unrounded counts, then applying fixed random noise directly to those unperturbed measures. This has the effect of making noise more consistent for measures, while being fixed and maintaining confidentiality.

### Rounding of measures rule changes

#### 2013 – Rule 8: Rounding of measures rule
All measures have simple conventional rounding applied.

#### 2018, 2023 – Rule removed

#### Explanation of changes
Rounding of measures is not done because it is not required.

### Release under license changes

#### 2013 – Release under license
Stats NZ may offer a licensed release of less well-confidentialised counts to institutions under strict conditions and only for internal institutional use.

#### 2018, 2023 – Rule removed

#### Explanation of changes
We reviewed our historic practices regarding the release of data under license and, since the 2018 Census, do not to release data under license.

## How we reviewed the confidentiality rules
In 2016 and 2017, we consulted with internal and external customers about their views on the 2013 Census confidentiality methods, and potential methods for the future.
We learned that external customers:
- wanted to create their own outputs online via an easy-to-use tool
- wanted outputs to be more detailed, multivariate, and customisable
- wanted less suppression of small-area and population data
- preferred noise to come in the form of random rounding to base 3 (RR3) rather than the uncertainty of adding noise in a less consistent manner
- considered additivity (counts in a table adding to the total listed) desirable but not a high priority, particularly if it comes at the expense of more suppression.
Internal customers generally agreed with these views and wanted more automation when applying confidentiality methods.
We used this feedback to develop new rules for 2018 Census.
A similar consultation process occurred between 2018 and 2023.
This consultation resulted in a decision to retain the 2018 Census rules, but to improve their implementation, and make it easier for census products and services and the customised data requests service to produce a wider range of pre-confidentialised tables for data customers.
To meet customers’ requirements for transparency and technical information, we are also publishing the technical methodological standard.
